items.text: crates/aion/src/durability/mod.rs declares the durability submodules and re-exports the public surface, containing no logic.

DurabilityError is a thiserror enum covering at least sequence/append failure (wrapping StoreError), non-determinism violation, and history-shape errors, with no panics or unwrap in its construction paths.
NonDeterminismError is a distinct typed error carrying the WorkflowId, the sequence position of the mismatch, the expected command shape, and the found recorded-event shape.
DurabilityError implements std::error::Error and Display via thiserror, and no library code path constructs an error by panicking.
A sequence-head tracker holds the current per-workflow head sequence and advances it only when an append succeeds.
Recorder is constructed for a single WorkflowId and is the only type that calls EventStore::append for that workflow.
Recorder computes expected_seq from its tracked head for every append; the head is never read from outside the Recorder nor guessed.

Recorder exposes recording methods for each event family: workflow lifecycle, activity (scheduled/started/completed/failed), timer (started/fired/cancelled), signal received, and child workflow (started/completed/failed).
When EventStore::append returns SequenceConflict, the Recorder surfaces a hard DurabilityError (double-writer bug) and does not retry by re-reading the head.
A Recorder can be initialised with a starting head read from existing history (so a recovered workflow continues appending at the correct sequence).
A correlation key type identifies a world-touching call: activity/child by scheduling ordinal (matching ActivityId/child WorkflowId derivation), timer by TimerId, signal by name-in-recorded-order.
HistoryCursor is built from an ordered Vec<Event> (read_history output) and walks it in sequence order.
HistoryCursor resolves the next recorded event matching a given event family and correlation key, advancing its internal position.
HistoryCursor reports exhaustion (no further recorded events) distinctly from a family/key mismatch.
Recorded non-exhausting ActivityFailed attempts are walked past as part of the recorded retry sequence so the cursor lands on the eventual recorded outcome.

A Command enum models the world-touching calls the workflow can issue: RunActivity, AwaitSignal, StartTimer, SpawnChild, plus workflow-completion intent, each carrying its correlation key and any input Payload.
A Resolution enum models recorded outcomes: ActivityCompleted(result), ActivityFailedTerminal(error), TimerFired, SignalDelivered(payload), ChildCompleted(result), ChildFailed(error).
The Resolver, given a Command, returns exactly one of: a recorded Resolution from the cursor, ResumeLive when the cursor is exhausted, or a NonDeterminismError on family/key mismatch.
All world-touching calls funnel through the single Resolver entry point; recorded-vs-live and violation are decided in exactly one place.
When the next recorded event differs in family or correlation key from the issued Command, the Resolver raises NonDeterminismError and never returns a Resolution for that command.
A non-determinism violation causes the workflow to fail deterministically: exactly one WorkflowFailed is recorded (via the Recorder) carrying the violation classification.

A dedicated test drives a recorded history that deliberately diverges from the replayed command stream (wrong activity family, wrong correlation key) and asserts the typed NonDeterminismError with correct expected/found shapes.
DeterminismContext exposes the recorded timestamp of the event currently being applied as the sole source for workflow.now; it advances as the Resolver consumes recorded events.
No code path in this cluster calls the wall clock (SystemTime::now / Utc::now) to produce a workflow-visible value.
DeterminismContext provides a deterministic RNG seeded solely from WorkflowId + RunId as the sole source for workflow.random.
Two DeterminismContexts constructed for the same WorkflowId + RunId produce identical random sequences (asserted by test).
A LiveExecutor trait defines the engine-implemented operations AD invokes only on resume-live: run an activity, start a timer, await a signal, spawn a child workflow.
While the cursor can satisfy a command, the LiveExecutor is never invoked (replay performs no live side effect).
After ResumeLive, the handoff glue invokes the LiveExecutor and records the resulting event(s) through the Recorder.
replay builds a Resolver from read_history and a DeterminismContext, and drives a freshly-created execution forward, satisfying each command from the cursor until exhaustion.
On replay a matched ActivityCompleted returns its recorded result Payload without dispatching the activity; a matched exhausted-retry ActivityFailed returns the recorded terminal ActivityError.
replay returns a resume point (the first command whose recorded event is absent) so the engine can continue live execution from there.

Behavioural tests over InMemoryStore assert: a fully-recorded history replays to its recorded terminal state with zero live calls; a partial history replays to the correct resume point; activity results are returned from cache.
recover calls EventStore::list_active and, for each returned WorkflowId, reads its history and replays it to its resume point.
recover initialises each workflow's Recorder at the head implied by its existing history so post-recovery appends continue at the correct sequence.
recover surfaces a per-workflow failure (e.g. a NonDeterminismError during replay) without silently dropping the workflow or aborting recovery of the others.
A test seeds InMemoryStore with multiple active and terminal workflows and asserts recover replays exactly the active ones to their correct resume points.

An integration test records a workflow's events via the Recorder, then replays from the same store and asserts the replayed Resolutions match the recorded outcomes in order and the resume point is correct.
An integration test asserts workflow.now values during replay equal the recorded event timestamps in order, and that random draws match across two independent replays of the same run.

A WorkflowReopened event variant exists on the Event enum, engine-internal (never authored by workflow code), carrying the reopened run id and the set of activity correlation keys to re-dispatch; it round-trips through serde and every store/wire layer like any other event.

status_from_events projects WorkflowReopened as Running, so a WorkflowReopened recorded after a terminal WorkflowFailed supersedes it under the existing last-lifecycle-event-wins scan, exactly as a replacement WorkflowStarted does for continue-as-new.

Terminal detection is consolidated onto one reset-aware predicate: a run is terminal iff a terminal event was recorded since its last reopen point (or run start), not merely if any terminal event exists in the run; every site (status projection, terminal-outcome, run-has-terminal, close-time, startup/list_active) uses it and agrees.

A workflow whose latest events are WorkflowFailed then WorkflowReopened is re-included by EventStore::list_active and by startup recovery (it projects Running) and is not skipped by the is_terminal guard.

The exactly-one-terminal guard (ensure_no_recorded_terminal and the process-exit terminal recorder) is scoped to the current lease: it still rejects a second terminal within one lease, but permits a new terminal after an intervening reopen point, preserving exactly-one-terminal-per-lease.

The history cursor treats a WorkflowReopened naming correlation key K as a reset point for K: attempts recorded for K before the reset are superseded, and replay resolves K to its next recorded attempt or, if none yet, reports K exhausted so the resolver returns ResumeLive for live re-dispatch.

After a reopen and a fresh recorded attempt for K, replay deterministically resolves K to the new attempt's recorded outcome on every subsequent replay, because the WorkflowReopened marker is itself recorded in append-only history.

The Recorder is the sole append path for WorkflowReopened via a single-writer builder method using the same WriteToken/expected-seq discipline as every other recorded event; no code path appends it directly to the store.

Reopen targets only activities that ended in a terminal failure with no later successful attempt in the run; merely in-flight (scheduled, no terminal) activities are not listed and continue to re-dispatch via the existing recovery path.
